result:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of silence that only exists just before something breaks.

It isn’t peaceful. It isn’t calm. It’s tight, like a held breath that has forgotten how to leave the body. The kind of silence that presses against your ears until you start to wonder if you’ve gone deaf, or if the world itself has decided to stop making noise.

I first noticed it on a Tuesday that pretended to be ordinary.

The sky was the exact shade of blue you’d expect from a sky that had no intention of being remembered. Cars moved when they were supposed to, people crossed streets with the quiet obedience of those who trust painted lines, and somewhere a dog barked in the distance, because of course it did. Everything was working. Everything was fine.

And yet.

There was that silence.

Not the absence of sound, but the absence of something underneath it. Like the world had forgotten to hum.

I was sitting at a bus stop I didn’t need to be at. That’s not as poetic as it sounds. I had simply gotten off my bus two stops early without realizing it, distracted by a thought I can no longer recall. By the time I noticed, the bus was already pulling away, its rear lights blinking like a slow, indifferent goodbye.

So I sat.

There’s something about bus stops that invites reflection in the least productive way possible. You don’t choose to be there, not really. You’re between places, suspended in a moment that doesn’t belong to you. It’s a good place to notice things you’d rather not.

Like the silence.

A man sat two seats down from me, scrolling through his phone with the intensity of someone searching for proof that something still mattered. His thumb moved in short, mechanical swipes. Every few seconds, he paused, frowned, and then resumed, as if disappointment had become part of the rhythm.

Across the street, a woman stood outside a bakery, staring through the glass. She wasn’t looking at anything in particular—just standing there, hands in her pockets, as though she had forgotten the next step in a simple process.

Go in. Order. Leave.

But she didn’t.

A breeze moved through the street, lifting a loose receipt from the ground and carrying it in a lazy arc before dropping it again. The paper landed near my shoe. I picked it up without thinking.

It wasn’t a receipt.

At least, not exactly.

There were numbers, yes, and a date, and something that might have been the name of a store if you squinted at it long enough. But the ink seemed… unsettled. Like it hadn’t decided what it wanted to say. The characters blurred at the edges, shifting slightly when I tried to focus on them, as if they were embarrassed to be seen.

I blinked, and the text snapped into place.

Milk. Bread. Eggs.

Normal. Completely normal.

I let out a small breath I didn’t realize I’d been holding. The silence pressed closer.

The man next to me stopped scrolling.

Not paused—stopped. His thumb hovered over the screen, frozen mid-motion. His eyes were still, locked onto whatever had been there a moment before. Then, slowly, he lowered the phone into his lap and looked up.

Not at anything.

Just up.

“Do you hear that?” he asked.

His voice didn’t break the silence. It folded into it, like a note played on an instrument tuned slightly wrong.

“Hear what?” I said.

He tilted his head, listening harder. “Exactly.”

I didn’t understand what he meant until I did.

The dog had stopped barking.

The cars were still moving, but without sound. Their tires turned against the asphalt in perfect, noiseless rotations. A cyclist passed by, pedaling with visible effort, yet there was no chain rattle, no breath, no friction.

The world had not gone quiet.

It had been muted.

The woman across the street finally moved. She reached for the bakery door, her hand closing around the handle. For a moment, she just held it there, as if waiting for permission.

Then she pushed.

The bell above the door didn’t ring.

She stepped inside, and for a brief second, I could see her reflection in the glass—perfectly clear, perfectly ordinary.

Then it lagged.

Her reflection was half a second behind her, like a video struggling to keep up with its own playback. She took another step, and the gap widened. The version of her in the glass moved slower, softer, as though resisting the act of following.

I stood up.

“Okay,” I said, though I wasn’t sure who I was saying it to. “Okay, that’s not—”

The man beside me grabbed my wrist.

His grip was firm, but not panicked. Grounding, almost.

“Don’t,” he said.

“Don’t what?”

“Move like you think everything’s still working.”

That would have been a strange sentence under any circumstance. Here, it felt like instruction.

I looked down at his hand on my wrist. It was solid. Real. The warmth of it was undeniable.

“Something’s wrong,” I said.

“Something’s missing,” he corrected.

We sat there for a moment longer, the two of us anchored to a bench that suddenly felt like the only reliable object in existence.

Then, from somewhere—not far, not near, just somewhere—there was a sound.

A single, sharp crack.

It didn’t echo. It didn’t reverberate. It simply existed, fully formed, and then it was gone.

The silence that followed was different.

Looser.

Like a knot had been untied.

The cars roared back to life all at once, a chaotic rush of engines and motion. The bell above the bakery door rang—late, but determined. The dog resumed barking, louder than before, as if trying to make up for lost time.

I pulled my wrist free, not because I wanted to, but because I needed to check that I still could.

The man exhaled, a long, steady release.

“Did you feel that?” I asked.

He nodded. “Yeah.”

“What was it?”

He picked up his phone again, stared at the screen for a moment, then locked it without scrolling.

“I think,” he said slowly, “we just noticed something we weren’t supposed to.”

That wasn’t comforting.

Across the street, the woman exited the bakery with a small paper bag. She looked normal again—completely, undeniably normal. If I hadn’t seen what I’d seen, I would have believed she had always been that way.

The receipt in my hand crinkled.

I looked down.

Milk. Bread. Eggs.

And, at the very bottom, in text so faint I wasn’t sure it would survive another glance:

Thank you for your patience.

I looked up, but the man was already standing.

“Wait,” I said. “What does that mean?”

He smiled, but it wasn’t reassuring. It was the kind of smile you give when you recognize a pattern you wish you didn’t.

“It means,” he said, stepping back as a bus pulled up to the curb, “whatever paused… just resumed.”

The doors hissed open.

People stepped off. People stepped on.

Everything was working.

Everything was fine.

And yet, as I boarded the bus, I couldn’t shake the feeling that somewhere, just beneath the noise and motion and ordinary Tuesday sky, something was still holding its breath.

Waiting for the next time it forgets to exhale.
:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
